extra.reasoning:
Property: NOT(rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b). rec_drug_a is set true ONLY by r3 (priority 50), whose condition is (qt_prolongation AND seizure_history); otherwise it defaults false. rec_drug_b is governed by r4 (priority 90: IF qt AND seizure THEN false), r6 (priority 50: IF qt THEN true), and default false.

For a violation we need rec_drug_a=true AND rec_drug_b=true. rec_drug_a=true forces qt_prolongation=true and seizure_history=true (the only rule, r3, requires this). But that same condition triggers r4 for rec_drug_b at priority 90, setting rec_drug_b:=false. r6 also fires (qt true) at priority 50 trying to set rec_drug_b:=true, but conflict resolution is highest-priority-wins per output, so r4 (90) beats r6 (50): rec_drug_b=false. Hence whenever rec_drug_a is true, rec_drug_b is necessarily false.

Conversely, rec_drug_b=true requires r6 to win, which needs qt=true and r4 NOT to fire, i.e., seizure_history=false. But then r3's condition fails so rec_drug_a=false. The two outputs can never be jointly true under any of the 32 input combinations. Note r3 and r4 share the identical guard (qt AND seizure), which is the key structural reason the higher-priority suppression of drug_b always co-occurs with the recommendation of drug_a. The property holds.
